verdicts.reasoning: The taint flow misidentifies the sink. The flagged expression '$request = wp_remote_post($this->api_url, [..., "body" => $api_params])' uses $this->api_url as the destination URL — this is a hardcoded value set at construction time from the plugin's adapter config (https://yaycommerce.com/). The user-controlled value $_REQUEST['slug'] after sanitize_text_field() flows only into the POST *body* parameter array ($api_params), not into the URL. WordPress's wp_remote_post() does not use body parameters as the destination URL. Additionally, show_changelog() is guarded by current_user_can('update_plugins'), restricting it to site administrators. No SSRF is present.

taint_flow_reanalysis.user_controlled_argument: POST body only — cannot redirect the HTTP request

additional_manual_findings.title: workHours startTime/endTime stored without sanitization
additional_manual_findings.reasoning: The daysOfWeekWorking[*][workHours][*][startTime] and endTime values from $_POST are stored raw in post meta via update_post_meta(). In meta-accounts.php (line 84/87), these are passed to Helper::buildTimeSelector() which only uses the $default parameter for a string equality comparison ('$time == $default') to determine whether to add 'selected' attribute. The $default value is never echoed into HTML directly; only the fixed H:i formatted time values from date() are interpolated into the option elements. No XSS is achievable through this path.

additional_manual_findings.title: AJAX handlers lack explicit capability check (rely on nonce only)
additional_manual_findings.reasoning: All AJAX handlers (save_display_setting, save_design_setting, save_woocommerce_setting, save_analytics_setting, save_url_setting, save_user_role_setting, set_account_position, load_accounts_ajax, set_account_status) are registered exclusively via 'wp_ajax_' hooks (not 'wp_ajax_nopriv_'), meaning WordPress itself enforces the login requirement. They rely on check_ajax_referer('njt-wa-nonce') but do not call current_user_can(). The nonce 'njt-wa-nonce' is only generated via admin_enqueue_scripts, which fires only on plugin-specific admin pages that already require 'nta_wa_manage' capability. A subscriber-level user cannot ordinarily obtain this nonce through the normal application flow. While any logged-in user can theoretically compute WordPress nonces for any action (the nonce seed depends only on user_id, session token, action, and time), this would require a targeted offline computation. The practical exploit path is insufficiently concrete to confirm as a live vulnerability.

additional_manual_findings.title: Dynamic post meta key via user-controlled $type in set_account_position and set_account_status
additional_manual_findings.reasoning: The $type parameter from $_POST is sanitized via sanitize_text_field() and interpolated into the meta key as 'nta_wa_{$type}'. This means an attacker with valid nonce access (authenticated admin) could write to any post meta key beginning with 'nta_wa_'. However: (1) post meta key injection cannot achieve code execution or privilege escalation through meta key manipulation alone; (2) the value ($index/$status) is also sanitized; (3) the nonce gate limits this to admin-context users. Low-impact meta pollution at most. Not a confirmable vulnerability.
